elements.sent-2411-210: Bayesian optimisation (BO) is a sequential global optimisation strategy used to tackle expensive-to-evaluate black-box functions.
elements.sent-2411-211: This optimisation strategy is based on Bayes' theorem, which provides a way to update probabilities of a hypothesis (or model), H, given evidence (data, or observations), E, is proportional to the likelihood of E given H, multiplied by the prior probability of H.
elements.sent-2411-212: Mathematically, \begin{equation}\text{P}(\text{H}|\text{E}) = \frac{\text{P}(\text{E}|\text{H})\text{P}(\text{H})}{\text{P}(\text{E})}\end{equation} In the context of BO, the prior represents the initial assumptions (such as smoothness, noise in observations, etc) about the objective function, $f$.
elements.sent-2411-213: The likelihood here quantifies how likely the observed data, O, is, given the prior, whereby the observed values are sampled data points of the true objective function.
elements.sent-2411-214: The combination of the prior with the evidence allows us to obtain the posterior distribution.
elements.sent-2411-215: In MOBO, the task involves concurrently optimising multiple objective functions.
elements.sent-2411-216: Mathematically, this can be expressed as \begin{equation}\text{max}(f_1(\hat{x}), f_2(\hat{x}), ..., f_p(\hat{x})) \; \;\;\; \hat{x} \in \mathbb{R}^q\end{equation} where $p$ is the number of objective functions, $f_i(i=1,...,p)$ are the objective functions, and $\hat{x}$ are the independent design parameters in $q$ dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-217: This assumes that the problem requires the maximisation of the objectives.
elements.sent-2411-218: Conversely, the problem can be transformed into a minimisation problem by negating the objective functions.
elements.sent-2411-219: In reality, these objectives may be conflicting, leading to a situation where it is improbable to maximise all objectives simultaneously, giving rise to the concept of Pareto optimality.
elements.sent-2411-220: A solution is considered Pareto optimal if no other solution would make any objective function better without compromising at least one other objective.
elements.sent-2411-221: By considering Pareto optimality, the trade-offs between conflicting objectives can be navigated, ultimately identifying a set of Pareto-optimal solutions.
elements.sent-2411-222: In the context of MOBO, Pareto-optimal solutions can also be referred to as non-dominated solutions within the solution space.
elements.sent-2411-223: Conversely, a solution is considered dominated if there exists another solution that surpasses it in at least one objective without worsening any other objective.
elements.sent-2411-224: The approaches to solve MOBO problems can be classified depending on whether weights are assigned to the objectives beforehand (known as scalarisation methods) or not (known as hypervolume (HV) methods).
elements.sent-2411-225: Regardless of the methods used, in MOBO, each objective function is approximated by an independent GP, which is used to represent the posterior distribution.
elements.sent-2411-226: A GP defines a probability distribution over functions, such that for any set of inputs in a continuous domain, the corresponding output values can be modelled as a joint Gaussian distribution \cite{rasmussen2003gaussian .}
elements.sent-2411-227: They are characterised by a mean function $m(x)$, and a covariance function $k(x, \bar{x )$, defined in terms of input vectors x, and $\bar{x}$.}
elements.sent-2411-228: For simplicity, the mean function can identically be zero, without losing generality \cite{books/lib/RasmussenW06 . \begin{equation}f(x) \sim \mathcal{GP}(m(x), k(x,x*))\end{equation} GPs can be trained using a given set of observations, $O = \left \{ x_i, \text{y _i \right \}^N_{i = 1}$, where $x_i \in \mathbb{R ^q$, given $N$ number of observations, and in a $q$ dimensional space.}}}
elements.sent-2411-229: In addition, assuming noisy observations, $\text{y _i = f(x_i) + \epsilon_i \in \mathbb{R}$, where the noise, $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N (0, \bar{\sigma^2_n )$, is mean-centered and has a variance $\bar{\sigma^2_n}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-230: Consequently, the Gaussian distribution can then be obtained using \begin{equation}y \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \mathbf{K}_{ij})\end{equation} where $\mu_i = m(x_i), i = 1, ..., n$, and $\mathbf{K _{ij = k(x_i, x_j), i,j = 1, ..., n$, forming the prior distribution.}}
elements.sent-2411-231: This distribution is independent of the training data but assumes a smooth objective function.
elements.sent-2411-232: Next, updating the prior requires the joint Gaussian distribution to be formed, assuming $y = \left \{\text{y _i \right \}^N_{i = 1}$ is the observed output of the training dataset, let $y_* = \left \{\text{y _i^* \right \}^N_{i = 1}$ be the outputs from the test set, with $X$ being the input for the training set, and $X^*$ being the input for the test set. \begin{equation}\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{y} \mathbf{y}_* \end{bmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N}\left( \mathbf{0}, \begin{bmatrix} K_X = k(X, X) + \bar{\sigma^2_n} I k(X, X^*) k(X, X^*)^T k(X^*, X^*) \end{bmatrix} \right)\end{equation} The predictive distribution over the test points is then given by \begin{equation}y_*|y \sim \mathcal{N}(k(X, X^*)^TK_Xy, k(X^*, X^*) - k(X, X^*)^TK_X^{-1}k(X, X^*))\end{equation} The mean and variance at a given input location $x^*$, with a training set $X$ can then be obtained using \begin{equation}\mu(x^*) = k(X, x^*)^T (K_X^{-1} y) \bar{\sigma^2}(x^*) = k(x^*, x^*) - k(X, x^*)^T (K_X^{-1} k(X, x^*))\end{equation} It is essential to choose a covariance function that is representative of the objective function, as it directly influences the output.}}
elements.formula-2411-015: \begin{equation} y y_*  \sim N\left( 0,  K_X = k(X, X) + \bar{\sigma^2_n} I k(X, X^*) k(X, X^*)^T k(X^*, X^*)  \right)\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-016: \begin{equation}y_*|y \sim N(k(X, X^*)^TK_Xy, k(X^*, X^*) - k(X, X^*)^TK_X^{-1}k(X, X^*))\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-017: \begin{equation}\mu(x^*) = k(X, x^*)^T (K_X^{-1} y) \bar{\sigma^2}(x^*) = k(x^*, x^*) - k(X, x^*)^T (K_X^{-1} k(X, x^*))\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-233: One such function is the radial basis function (RBF), also known as the squared exponential \begin{equation}k(x', x'') = \theta_0^2 \cdot \exp\left(\ - \frac{\| x' - x''\|^2}{2\ell^2}\right)\end{equation} $\ell$ is the length scale, which controls the smoothness of the kernel function.
elements.sent-2411-234: The hyperparameter $\theta_0$ controls the amplitude of the kernel function, whereby it determines the amount of variation introduced by the function being modelled.
elements.sent-2411-235: These are kernel parameters that are optimised by the GP model by maximising the likelihood over the observed dataset, which requires the use of gradient-based optimisers such as L-BFGS-B or Adam.
elements.sent-2411-236: The numerator, $\| x' - x''\|^2$, represents the Euclidean distance between the input points $x'$ and $x''$.
elements.sent-2411-237: Other kernel functions include the Exponential, Matern kernel functions, etc. To be able to guide the sequential optimisation, acquisition functions play a pivotal role in effectively navigating the design space and finding the global optimum of the objective function.
elements.sent-2411-238: One example of such a function is the probability of improvement (PI), whereby a point is selected based on the highest probability of improvement over the current maximum \cite{wilson2018maximizing .}
elements.sent-2411-239: Assuming that the function to be maximised is $f(x)$ and the current maximum is $f(x^+)$, thus the `improvement' can be defined as \begin{equation}\text{PI}(x) = \text{P}(f(x) > f(x^+)) = \Phi\left ( \frac{\mu(x) - f(x^+) }{\bar{\sigma}(x)}\right )\end{equation} where $\Phi$ is the cumulative distribution function of the standard normal distribution, and $\bar{\sigma}$ is the standard deviation.
elements.sent-2411-240: By maximising the value of the acquisition function, the best samples can be found that optimise the objective values and reduce the uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-241: This is an iterative process, allowing the objective space to be efficiently explored by considering the accumulated data.
elements.sent-2411-242: With the increased computational effort as a result of an increasing number of objectives, scalarisation methods provide an effective alternative.
elements.sent-2411-243: These methods attempt to convert multiple objectives into a single objective function effectively.
elements.sent-2411-244: Some of the scalarisation techniques include using the weighted sum method \cite{marler2010weighted , the weighted norm method, used in multi-objective Evolutionary Algorithm based on Decomposition (MOEA/D) \cite{zhang2007moea , using the Chebyshev function and its extension the Augmented Chebyshev function that is normally used in efficient global optimisation algorithms (ParEGO) \cite{knowles2006parego .}}}
elements.sent-2411-245: This paper focuses on utilising the ParEGO method to obtain a set of Pareto optimal solutions.
elements.sent-2411-246: ParEGO assigns weights randomly with a uniform distribution, in our case, sampling a uniform simplex.
elements.sent-2411-247: The ParEGO method works by applying the augmented Chebyshev scalarisation, which is given by: \begin{equation}g(y)=\max_i[w_i|f_i-z_i^*|]+\alpha\sum_{i=1}^k|f_i-z_i^*|\end{equation} where $w_i$ are weights that are randomly assigned to the $i$th objective, $z_i^*$ is an ideal/utopian objective vector that is unattainable.
elements.sent-2411-248: As the objectives are normalised to values between [0, 1], $z_i^*$ becomes a vector of zeros, and $\alpha = 0.05$.
elements.sent-2411-250: The scaled function $g(y)$ is then minimised, with GPs being used to approximate the scaled function.
elements.sent-2411-251: An alternative method is the weighted sum, whereby random weights are assigned to the objectives and combined to form a singular objective function.
elements.sent-2411-252: Mathematically, this can expressed as \begin{equation}g(\mathbf{y}) = \sum_{i=1}^{k}w_if_i\end{equation} which is similar to the formulation of the Chebyshev scalarisation, but this method also considers the magnitude of all objectives.
elements.sent-2411-253: Subsequently, both scalarisation techniques are used with a singular GP as a surrogate and an acquisition function such as the Expected Improvement (EI).
elements.sent-2411-254: In this study, the log-EI function has been utilised, which is an extension of the EI function, expressed as \begin{equation}\text{logEI}(x) = \log(\mathbb{E}(\max(f(x) - f(x^+),0))) \label{equ:log ei}\end{equation} This is derived from the probability of improvement acquisition function.
elements.sent-2411-255: Finding the expectation of the results (i.e.~multiplying by the probability of the outcomes) in the formulation of the expected improvement acquisition function.
elements.sent-2411-256: The logarithm function ensures numerical stability, which is especially important in automatic differentiation.
elements.sent-2411-257: However, for MOBO, more complex acquisition functions are required to traverse the design space.
elements.sent-2411-258: While the computation of these MOBO acquisition functions is expensive, it allows effective exploration across the Pareto front.
elements.sent-2411-259: In the hypervolume approaches, the Hypervolume Indicator (HV) is utilised, which is used to evaluate the quality of a Pareto front approximation set, $\mathcal{P}$.
elements.sent-2411-260: This approach measures the size of the dominated space bounded from below by a reference point r \cite{zitzler1999multiobjective .}
elements.sent-2411-261: This reference point is generally chosen by the user, in a manner that satisfies the condition that it is dominated by all objectives, i.e.~a nadir point.
elements.sent-2411-262: Mathematically, the HV of a set $\mathcal{P}$, where $\mathcal{P = \{ y_{(1) ,...,y_{(i)}\} \subset \mathbb{R ^d$, is defined as the $d$-dimensional Lebesgue measure, $\lambda_d$, where $d$ corresponds to the number of objectives, and $i$ corresponds to the number of Pareto optimal points, here a bi-objective problem is being solved, hence $d = 2$, consequently, $y = \{f_1, f_2\}$ with $f_1$ and $f_2$ being two objectives in question: \begin{equation}\text{HV}(\mathcal{P}) = \lambda_2(\cup_{y \in \mathcal{P}}[r, y_i])\end{equation} Where r and $y_i$ represent the vertices of a rectangle.}}}
elements.sent-2411-263: By extension, the Hypervolume Improvement (HVI) can be calculated, which considers the improvement in the HV for a new observation, $y_*$: \begin{equation}\text{HVI}(\mathcal{P}, y, r) = \text{HV}(\mathcal{P} \cup y_*, r) - \text{HV}(\mathcal{P}, r)\end{equation} Fig.~\ref{fig:pareto_front_and_hv_explanation (a) shows the improvement in the hypervolume as a result of observing a new value in the objective space.}
elements.formula-2411-024: \begin{equation}HVI(P, y, r) = HV(P \cup y_*, r) - HV(P, r)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-264: However, to consider the magnitude of the improvement, the expectation of HVI must be calculated, allowing the Expected Hypervolume Improvement (EHVI) to be calculated.
elements.sent-2411-265: This is defined as: \begin{equation}\text{EHVI}(\mathbf{\mu}, \mathbf{\sigma}, \mathcal{P}, r) = \int_{\mathbb{R}^2}\text{HVI}(\mathcal{P}, y, r) \cdot \phi_{\mu,\bar{\sigma}}(y) dy \label{equ:EHVI}\end{equation} Where $\phi_{\mu,\bar{\sigma }$ represents the probability density function for a multivariate normal distribution with mean values of $\mu \in \mathbb{R ^d$ and standard deviation of $\bar{\sigma \in \mathbb{R ^d_+$, as shown by Yang et al.~\cite{yang2019multi .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-266: However, the analytical calculation for the HVI can be computationally expensive.
elements.sent-2411-267: To remedy this, a fast partitioning method can be used, which partitions the non-dominated space into $n + 1$ disjointed rectangles $S_k$ (in 2D), in tandem with piece-wise integration \cite{emmerich2016multicriteria .}
elements.sent-2411-268: Each disjoint rectangle is defined by a pair of upper and lower vertices, $\text{u _i$, and $\text{l _i$, respectively, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:pareto_front_and_hv_explanation (b).}}}
elements.sent-2411-269: By adding two additional points: $y_0 = (\text{r _1, \infty)^T$, and $y_{n+1 = (\infty, \text{r _2)^T$, where $r = (r_1, r_2)$, then the disjoint rectangles can be defined as \begin{equation}S_i = \left ( \left ( f_2^{(i)}, -\infty)^T, ( f_1^{(i-1)}, -f_2^{(i)})^T \right ) \right ) \; \; i = 1,...,n+1\end{equation}.}}}
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{new_images/pareto_front_and_hv_explanation.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:pareto_front_and_hv_explanation} (a) Illustration of Hypervolume Improvement (HVI) in a two-dimensional objective space with objectives $f_1$, and $f_2$. The Pareto front (Red line) $\mathcal{P}$ consists of a set of evaluated points $\{ \text{y}_1, \text{y}_2, \text{y}_3\}$, which are the non-dominated solutions. The dominated solutions, represented by $\{ \text{y}_4, \text{y}_5\}$, lie within the dominated solutions region. The yellow region indicates the HVI resulting from evaluating a new point $\text{y}_*$. (b) Visualisation of fast partitioning of the non-dominated space for efficient Expected Hypervolume Improvement (EHVI) computation in 2-D using box decomposition. Each disjointed rectangle is labelled $S_i$, and is defined by its upper and lower vertices $u_i$ and $l_i$. Finally, $\mathbf{r}$ refers to the reference point from which the hypervolume indicator is calculated.}
elements.sent-2411-270: Defining a function $\Delta$, which denotes the subset of objective vectors $y \in \mathbb{R ^2$, $\Delta(y, \mathcal{P , r)$ are dominated by a vector $y$ but are not dominated by $\mathcal{P}$, but dominate the reference point $r$, as shown by the grey region in Fig.~\ref{fig:pareto_front_and_hv_explanation , the HVI in 2-D is then given by: \begin{equation}\text{HVI}(\mathcal{P}, y, r) = \sum_{i=1}^{n+1}\lambda_2[S_i \cap \Delta(y, \mathcal{P}, r)] \label{equ:disjoint rectangles}\end{equation} Eqs.~(\ref{equ:EHVI ) and (\ref{equ:disjoint rectangles ) can then be combined for the efficient computation of EHVI acquisition function: \begin{equation}\text{EHVI}(\mathbf{\mu}, \mathbf{\sigma}, \mathcal{P}, r) = \int_{f_1=-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{f_2=-\infty}^{\infty}\sum_{i=1}^{n+1}\lambda_2[S_i \cap \Delta(y, \mathcal{P}, r)] \cdot \phi_{\mu,\bar{\sigma}}(y) dy\end{equation} Further simplifications can be found in \cite{yang2019multi .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-271: The MOBO framework comprises two key components: a Python wrapper that leverages the BoTorch package to perform BO with pre-built functions \cite{balandat2020botorch , and a MATLAB-ABAQUS interface designed for generating and meshing spinodoid topologies, followed by FEM analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-272: Within this framework, multiple MOBO-based methods have been implemented, each addressing the objectives in distinct ways: the hypervolume-based EHVI method, two scalarisation-based approaches - ParEGO and the weighted-sum method - and a comparison with random sampling using the Sobol' sequence.
elements.sent-2411-273: Fig.~\ref{fig:MOBO framework illustrates the various steps involved in the MOBO framework to generate solutions for the given problem.}
elements.sent-2411-274: Initially, independent GPs are configured for each objective using the GPyTorch package, in combination with the multi-output GP function ModelListGP, to establish the prior distribution.
elements.sent-2411-275: The prior is then updated using an initial dataset to form the posterior, thereby creating an initial surrogate model.
elements.sent-2411-276: However, to balance the exploration and exploitation by guiding the optimisation process, acquisition functions are used to obtain input points from the design points to evaluate next.
elements.sent-2411-277: BoTorch allows the use of two extensions of the EHVI and logEI (used for scalarisation methods) acquisition functions, specifically qNEHVI and qlogNEI.
elements.sent-2411-278: The qlogNEI acquisition function, which is an extension of the acquisition function as described by Eq.~(\ref{equ:log ei ), is designed to account for noise in observations.}
elements.sent-2411-279: In scenarios where functions are not deterministic, a single best value cannot be determined, qlogNEI integrates over all possible values to identify the next best point for sampling \cite{ament2024unexpected .}
elements.sent-2411-280: Similarly, qNEHVI assumes that the points observed on the Pareto front represent noisy realisations of the true Pareto front.
elements.sent-2411-281: Consequently, qNEHVI and qlogNEI require integration over the uncertainty of the in-sample (observed) data introduced.
elements.sent-2411-282: In addition, the two extensions support parallel evaluation of q-batch candidate points.
elements.sent-2411-283: Each candidate is selected considering the joint improvement from multiple candidate points.
elements.sent-2411-284: Moreover, asynchronous evaluation is possible, allowing the optimisation process to proceed while awaiting the remaining results of the q-batch samples.
elements.sent-2411-285: The acquisition function is conditioned on the pending points when selecting the next point to evaluate \cite{daulton2020differentiable .}
elements.sent-2411-286: Additionally, these acquisition functions utilise MC methods, which improve computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-287: Specifically, MC samples are drawn from the posterior distribution to approximate the expectation, and subsequently, the acquisition function.
elements.sent-2411-288: It is important to note that when observations are noiseless, these acquisition functions behave similarly to conventional acquisition functions.
elements.sent-2411-289: In higher-dimensional spaces, acquisition functions often become extremely complex, making analytical solutions impractical.
elements.sent-2411-290: As a result, direct solutions are generally unattainable, necessitating the use of numerical methods for optimising the acquisition function.
elements.sent-2411-291: A commonly used approach is the multi-start LBFGS-B algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-292: This second-order method relies on gradient and Hessian matrix calculations to locate the maximum of the acquisition function.
elements.sent-2411-293: A simple heuristic is applied to initialise the optimisation process, selecting 10 initial restart locations from a set of 512 randomly generated samples across the search space.
elements.sent-2411-294: Here, a restart location refers to the starting point at which the optimisation algorithm begins its search, helping to avoid getting stuck at a local optimum.
elements.sent-2411-295: Furthermore, the MC-based acquisition functions were approximated using 256 MC samples, corresponding to the accuracy of the posterior approximation, whereby the higher the value is the greater the accuracy at the cost of increased computation time.
elements.sent-2411-296: As stated in Sec.~\ref{subsection: hypervolume methods , hypervolume-based methods such as qNEHVI require a reference point to compute the HV (shown by the point on the bottom left of Fig.~\ref{fig:pareto_front_and_hv_explanation ).}}
elements.sent-2411-297: A typical rule of thumb used to prescribe a value for the reference point is choosing a point that is slightly worse than the lower bound of the objectives, since the problem that is being solved assumes that objectives are required to be maximised.
elements.sent-2411-298: However, many different methods exist that can be applied to select a reference point, especially when no knowledge of the solution domain exists, especially the lower bound of the objectives.
elements.sent-2411-299: To demonstrate the implementation of the MOBO framework, two benchmark test problems were used: a) the Branin-Currin problem with added observation noise for each objective, and b) the ZDT1 with no additional observational noise.
elements.sent-2411-301: Five methods were tested, including the noiseless qEHVI function, to compare its effectiveness against its noisy counterpart qNEHVI.
elements.sent-2411-302: Additionally, a comparison was made between two different kernels - the RBF and Matern kernels - for calculating the covariance matrix.
elements.sent-2411-303: Before optimisation, six samples were generated from the design space to form the initial dataset, with the maximum number of iterations set at 200.
elements.sent-2411-304: The HV was used to assess the quality of the solutions provided by each method.
elements.sent-2411-305: The benchmark studies led to the following conclusions: 1) Regardless of the problem type, the Matern kernel outperformed the RBF kernel by capturing more intricate relationships between neighbouring points, allowing most methods to converge faster and achieve a total hypervolume closer to the maximum hypervolume.
elements.sent-2411-306: Consequently, the Matern kernel was selected for solving the spinodoid structure optimisation problem.
elements.sent-2411-307: However, in the noiseless problem, both qEHVI and qNEHVI performed similarly, suggesting that qNEHVI is generally preferable.
elements.sent-2411-308: Thus, qNEHVI was one of the methods employed in the study of spinodoid cellular structures.
elements.sent-2411-309: Further details on the benchmark studies can be found in \ref{appendix:Benchmarks .}
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{new_images/MOBO_framework2.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:MOBO framework}A schematic of the multi-objective Bayesian optimisation (MOBO) process for optimising the spinodoid cellular structure design space involves four steps of the data-driven process \cite{bessa2017framework,shin2022spiderweb}. (1) Initial Dataset Creation: Sampling 50 points from the design space using the Sobol' sequence and evaluate them with FEM simulations to build the initial dataset. (2) Surrogate Model Update: Updating the Gaussian Process model based on the dataset to predict structural properties. (3) Identifying samples to evaluate: Using an acquisition function to identify and evaluate the most promising design points. (4) FEM Simulations: Performing FEM analysis on generated structures and extracting objectives to then update the dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-310: With the BO framework discussed and tested in Subsec.~\ref{subsec:BO Framework , it is crucial to emphasize the second part of the MOBO process, which involves integrating the MATLAB-ABAQUS interface to complete the optimisation cycle, as visualised in Fig.~\ref{fig:MOBO framework .}}
elements.sent-2411-311: The initial dataset for the BO process was created by sampling 50 data points using the Sobol' sequence, which was subsequently evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-312: The pair plot in \ref{appendix:Pair plot of initial dataset demonstrates the relationships between each design input and objective, highlighting a positive correlation between EA and PF.}
elements.sent-2411-313: Whilst the integration streamlines the design exploration process, achieving optimal performance also requires addressing critical structural behaviours that could compromise the functionality of the design.
elements.sent-2411-314: One such phenomenon is densification, which necessitates implementing filtering techniques to exclude undesirable designs, thereby ensuring that the optimisation process delivers practical and reliable solutions.
elements.subsubsection-2411-001: Gradient-Based Filtering to Address Densification in Spinodoid Structures
elements.sent-2411-315: Densification refers to the process where a structure's density increases under compressive forces, leading to a significant rise in stiffness.
elements.sent-2411-316: This increased stiffness reduces the structure's ability to absorb energy, as it becomes incapable of deforming easily, leading to a rapid increase in the force transmitted through the structure.
elements.sent-2411-317: In crashworthiness applications, structures are designed to prolong the duration in which the energy is being absorbed.
elements.sent-2411-318: This is primarily done by dissipating energy over a longer distance.
elements.sent-2411-319: However, a densified material shortens the duration of energy absorption, resulting in rapid deceleration, which is undesired.
elements.sent-2411-320: As a consequence, it is crucial to avoid selecting designs that densify within the working conditions, in this case at strain levels below 50\%.
elements.sent-2411-321: This necessitates the implementation of a process to filter out designs that exhibit such behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-322: To effectively filter these designs, it is essential to first identify what constitutes densification.
elements.sent-2411-323: In this study, linear regression is used to address this issue, with polynomial curve fitting applied within the MATLAB-ABAQUS pipeline using MATLAB's built-in polyfit function.
elements.sent-2411-324: This function returns the coefficients for the nth-degree polynomial that best fits the y data, thus minimising the sum of squares.
elements.sent-2411-325: These coefficients are then used to recreate the fitted lines.
elements.sent-2411-326: Here, data points within the strain interval between 0.2 and 0.5 are used for curve fitting.
elements.sent-2411-327: Fig.~\ref{fig:comparison_1st_vs_2nd_order_polynomial illustrates a comparison between first-order and second-order polynomials fitted to these data points, shown by the red and blue lines, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-328: The fit's quality is visually assessed by how closely these lines match the target behaviour, represented by the solid black line, which shows the typical behaviour of a structure undergoing densification.
elements.sent-2411-329: Densification on a Force-Displacement (F-D) curve manifests as an exponential rise in the force required to further deform the structure.
elements.sent-2411-330: The rate at which the force rises with an increasing displacement can be quantified using gradients, which has been shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:doe_gradient_filtering .}
elements.sent-2411-331: The results of applying the first derivative to the first-order polynomial line of best fit are shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:doe_gradient_filtering (a)(i).}
elements.sent-2411-332: The first derivative here reflects the rate of change, which is the stiffness on an F-D curve.
elements.sent-2411-333: However, these values do not indicate or highlight significant peaks, which would signal areas of greatest change i.e. maximum stiffness.
elements.sent-2411-334: Additionally, the gradient values are all positive due to the elastic-plastic constitutive material model used in the FEM analysis, meaning fractures do not occur, hence stiffness cannot be reduced.
elements.sent-2411-335: As a result, the first derivative method is relatively insensitive and ineffective at detecting rapid changes in the stiffness.
elements.sent-2411-336: To remedy this, the second derivative of the second-order polynomial was used, as it measures the rate of change in stiffness.
elements.sent-2411-337: For example, a positive second derivative would suggest that a structure is stiffening at an accelerating rate, which is indicative of densification.
elements.sent-2411-338: This has been shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:doe_gradient_filtering (a)(ii), where certain DoEs display very high second derivative values, which were not captured by the first-order polynomial filter.}
elements.sent-2411-339: To demonstrate the effectiveness of the second-order polynomial filtering, various points were selected based on their second derivative values annotated as A through E, with a reference line showing solid black representing the behaviour of a structure not undergoing densification.
elements.sent-2411-340: The corresponding F-D curves for these points are plotted in Fig.~\ref{fig:doe_gradient_filtering (b).}
elements.sent-2411-341: To differentiate between structures undergoing densification and those that are not, a threshold value is necessary.
elements.sent-2411-342: In this case, a second derivative value of 350 was used as the cut-off, above which structures were assumed to be undergoing densification.
elements.sent-2411-343: This threshold was determined by plotting the annotated points in Fig.~\ref{fig:doe_gradient_filtering (b) and comparing them to the reference line.}
elements.sent-2411-344: Cases A to D exhibit a rapid increase in the force during the latter stages of crushing, in contrast to the reference and case E, which show a more gradual increase in force.
elements.sent-2411-345: Nonetheless, a lower threshold, closer to 274, could be chosen if stricter filtering were to be required.
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{new_images/comparison_1st_vs_2nd_order_polynomial.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:comparison_1st_vs_2nd_order_polynomial}Comparison between 1st order and 2nd order polynomial lines used to fit the F-D curve.}
elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{new_images/doe_gradient_filtering.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:doe_gradient_filtering}(a) Plots illustrating the application of a gradient-based filter to 631 Design of Experiments (DoEs), with each evaluated point represented. (i) This plot displays the first derivative applied to the F-D curve, which is fitted using a first-order polynomial. (ii) This plot presents the second derivative values obtained from fitting the F-D curve with a second-order polynomial. The red horizontal line indicates the threshold above which designs are considered to be undergoing densification. (b) This subplot showcases select F-D curves from various designs with differing second derivative values, with each curve corresponding to the annotations found in (a)(ii).}
elements.sent-2411-346: It should be noted that the gradient-based filter was also applied to the initial dataset, with one of the points within the dataset deemed to have undergone densification when the filter had been applied.
elements.sent-2411-347: Nevertheless, direct optimisation of EA and PF in their current form is infeasible, as the MOBO framework requires both objectives to be maximised.
elements.sent-2411-348: To address this, the following scalings were applied to the normalised objective values, resulting in a convex-shaped Pareto front \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \overline{\text{EA}'} = \ln(\overline{\text{EA}}) \overline{\text{PF}'} = -\overline{\text{PF}} \label{equ:scaling} \end{aligned}\end{equation} By applying these scalings in Eq.~(\ref{equ:scaling ), the minimisation problem is effectively converted into a maximisation problem.}
elements.sent-2411-349: This approach was found to be more effective than directly maximising EA and assuming that the inverse of PF should also be maximised.
elements.sent-2411-350: The scalings enhanced the traversal across the Pareto front, optimising the effectiveness of the acquisition function.
elements.sent-2411-351: Given that this study focuses on optimising spinodoid cellular structures for crush energy absorption applications, where the goal is to maximise energy absorption while minimising peak force, FEM analysis and problem setup are crucial components of the optimisation process.
elements.sent-2411-352: Although EA and PF are the primary objectives, additional objectives could be considered if data is available for each structure, such as material weight (by calculating volume or density) or reusability (with a more comprehensive material constitutive model that accounts for hysteresis).
elements.sent-2411-353: The MATLAB-ABAQUS interface facilitates this process by generating a series of input files, including part geometries (topology, anvil, and loader), an assembly file detailing the positioning of each part, the material model with its specific coefficients, and a settings file defining boundary conditions and history plots.
elements.sent-2411-354: These files are created once the next evaluation point is identified.
elements.sent-2411-355: ABAQUS is then executed via the command line on MATLAB to run the FEM simulation.
elements.sent-2411-356: After the simulation is completed, MATLAB is employed for post-processing, extracting the F-D data for further analysis.
elements.sent-2411-357: To ensure the robustness of the optimisation process, several fail-safes were implemented.
elements.sent-2411-358: If a simulation fails due to issues in generating the spinodoid structure, a random point from the Sobol' sequence is sampled as a replacement, and this is repeated until a valid output is obtained.
elements.sent-2411-359: Additionally, a constraint was introduced to prevent optimal structures from densifying within the 50\% strain interval.
elements.sent-2411-360: This was achieved by applying a gradient-based filter to the extracted F-D data, classifying designs as either undergoing 'progressive failure' or 'densification'.
elements.sent-2411-361: Structures classified as having gone densification are automatically considered dominated solutions, with output values for EA and PF set to $(-4, -0.2)$.
elements.sent-2411-362: These values have also been applied when the acquisition function recommends a design with all three conical angles being zero, as it is not possible to generate such a structure.
elements.sent-2411-363: In this study, sequential optimisation was performed with $q=1$.
elements.sent-2411-364: The stochastic nature of spinodoid topologies, due to GRFs and random initialisations, results in varying microstructures and randomness in the measured mechanical properties, introducing noise into the observations.
elements.sent-2411-365: This makes acquisition functions that can handle noisy data particularly suitable for this problem.
elements.sent-2411-366: However, to ensure repeatability, a seed can be set for the random number generator, in this case, it was set to rng(123) in MATLAB, producing consistent structures and eliminating noise.
elements.sent-2411-367: Nevertheless, in this study, the reference point was determined after multiple runs with various reference points, eventually settling on $\mathbf{r = (-10, -0.3)$.}
elements.sent-2411-368: The optimisation loop was executed for a maximum of 250 iterations to ensure that a sufficiently populated Pareto front could be constructed, which also served as the stopping criterion.
elements.sent-2411-369: The full optimisation history can be found in \ref{appendix:Optimisation history .}
elements.sent-2411-370: The performance of the MOBO methods was evaluated against the NSGA-II algorithm by comparing the final hypervolume encompassed by the non-dominated solutions.
elements.sent-2411-371: The NSGA-II algorithm was implemented using the Python-based pymoo package, with the initial population derived from the initial dataset.
elements.sent-2411-372: The algorithm was run for 5 generations, resulting in a total of 250 evaluations ($50 \times 5 = 250$) \cite{blank2020pymoo .}
elements.sent-2411-373: Simulated Binary Crossover (SBX) was used to generate offspring, while Polynomial Mutation was applied to introduce small variations in gene values, thus promoting diversity in subsequent generations.
elements.sent-2411-374: It should be noted that NSGA-II was used as a black box; further tuning of the crossover and mutation rates may improve the results.
elements.sent-2411-375: Specific hardware information, which was used to run the optimisation tasks on the cluster can be found in \ref{appendix:HPC hardware specifications .}
elements.figure-2411-014: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{new_images/optimisation_results_hypervolume.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:optimisation_results_hypervolume}(a) Comparison of the hypervolume achieved by each MOBO method at each iteration of the optimisation process, starting from the dataset used to initialise the Gaussian Process surrogate model. The comparison continues until reaching the 250-iteration threshold. The hypervolume values for each method include the upper bound, median, and lower bound. The thicker lines depict the median values, while the thinner lines above and below represent the upper and lower bounds, respectively. (b) A bar chart comparing the final hypervolume obtained by various MOBO methods and the traditional NSGA-II method after 250 iterations.}
elements.sent-2411-376: Following the MOBO framework setup described in Sec.~\ref{sec:mobo framework , four MOBO methods were employed to obtain a set of Pareto-optimal designs, with NSGA-II - a traditional method - serving as a benchmark.}
elements.sent-2411-377: Fig.~\ref{fig:optimisation_results_hypervolume (a) presents the hypervolume of the non-dominated solutions, calculated at each iteration beyond the initial dataset, for all implemented MOBO methods.}
elements.sent-2411-378: The qNEHVI method outperforms other MOBO methods, as evidenced by a rapid rise in hypervolume after a few iterations, with a further significant increase at around 150 iterations.
elements.sent-2411-379: This suggests qNEHVI discovered optimal designs not found by other methods, resulting in a superior Pareto front.
elements.sent-2411-380: The weighted sum method also showed a strong early performance, with a steep increase in hypervolume up to 50 iterations, followed by gradual improvements until around 125 iterations, and a slight increase thereafter.
elements.sent-2411-381: Chebyshev scalarisation performed similarly to the weighted sum method but lacked the initial rapid improvement, showing a gradual increase in hypervolume starting around 80 iterations.
elements.sent-2411-382: As expected, all three methods outperformed the random Sobol' sequence sampling.
elements.sent-2411-383: Fig.~\ref{fig:optimisation_results_hypervolume (b) displays the final hypervolume calculated after 250 iterations, including results from NSGA-II.}
elements.sent-2411-384: While NSGA-II achieved a higher hypervolume than random Sobol' sequence sampling, the weighted sum, and Chebyshev scalarisation methods, it was outperformed by the qNEHVI method.
elements.sent-2411-385: The poorer performance of Chebyshev scalarisation can be attributed to its suitability for non-convex Pareto fronts, unlike the convex Pareto front observed in this study.
elements.sent-2411-386: The weighted sum method, being straightforward, is better suited to handling convex Pareto fronts but lacks the targeted hypervolume improvement and exploration-exploitation balance of qNEHVI.
elements.sent-2411-387: A random combination of weights in the weighted sum and Chebyshev scalarisation methods may lead to overexploitation of certain regions, affecting the quality of the surrogate model.
elements.sent-2411-388: In the case of Augmented Chebyshev, the method might overly focus on regions near the ideal point, resulting in insufficient exploration.
elements.sent-2411-389: NSGA-II, while competitive, may not explore the objective space efficiently if mutation and crossover rates and other hyperparameters are not fine-tuned, and it often requires many generations to converge to the true Pareto front, which can be computationally expensive.
elements.sent-2411-390: In contrast, qNEHVI is more effective overall, requiring minimal hyperparameter tuning and specifically targeting hypervolume improvement.
elements.figure-2411-015: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{new_images/pareto_optimal_solutions.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:pareto_optimal_solutions}The pareto-optimal solutions obtained using the hypervolume-based qNEHVI method are visualised alongside selected example points, indicated by the coloured markers. Subplot (a) illustrates the scaled objectives, adjusted according to Eq.~(\ref{equ:scaling}), while subplot (b) presents the unscaled results in the normalised objective plane with points in red showing the initial dataset. Subplot (c) shows the topologies corresponding to the respective objective values, represented by the differently coloured markers in (a) and (b). Finally, subplot (d) depicts the F-D curves of the chosen structures undergoing compression.}
elements.sent-2411-391: Fig.~\ref{fig:pareto_optimal_solutions (a) illustrates the set of Pareto-optimal solutions derived from the qNEHVI method.}
elements.sent-2411-392: The Pareto front includes 78 designs, each contributing uniquely to the overall hypervolume, Fig.~\ref{fig:pareto_optimal_solutions (b) maps the rescaled Pareto-optimal points onto the original normalised objective plane, also displaying the initial dataset for comparison.}
elements.sent-2411-393: Compared to the initial dataset, the MOBO method has identified several optimal designs that exhibit reduced PF while preserving the EA capability.
elements.sent-2411-394: Moreover, qNEHVI has uncovered previously undiscovered optimal points at the higher end of the EA range, particularly in the upper-right region of the normalised PF-EA graph.
elements.sent-2411-395: Six optimal points were selected to demonstrate the robustness of these designs, with Fig.~\ref{fig:pareto_optimal_solutions (c) showcasing the generated topologies, featuring various microstructures with differing relative densities.}
elements.sent-2411-396: The F-D behaviour of each selected design under compression is plotted in Fig.~\ref{fig:pareto_optimal_solutions (d), revealing distinct performance characteristics.}
elements.sent-2411-397: It is important to note that high PF values have not been observed in the optimal solutions, which are predominantly found in anisotropic structures, such as the `Columnar'-type demonstrated in the experimental validation shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:PET-G experimental validation .}
elements.sent-2411-398: Moreover, the optimal designs predominantly feature anisotropic structures, particularly those oriented perpendicular to the crushing direction such as `Lamellar'-type, as well as structures with mixed geometries that lack distinct characteristics or any correlation with conical angles.
elements.sent-2411-399: These are examples of bending-dominated structures, where loads are primarily resisted through bending and flexural mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-400: While these structures tend to have lower stiffness and strength due to localised deformations that can lead to early failure through buckling or yielding, the bending mechanisms enable stress distribution across the structure.
elements.sent-2411-401: This allows the load to be spread over a larger area as the structure deforms more easily, effectively delaying catastrophic failure, which results in such structures having higher EA.
elements.sent-2411-402: It should also be noted that these solutions were deemed to not have undergone densification, which is also evident in the F-D curves of the selected cases.
elements.figure-2411-016: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{new_images/conditional_optimal_solutions_figure.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:conditional_optimal_solutions_figure}Illustration of subplots showcasing a set of solutions generated by applying various design constraints to the Pareto-optimal solutions obtained using the qNEHVI method. (a) Displays solutions with (i) low relative density, $\rho = 0.3$, (ii) high relative density, $\rho = 0.6$. Additional constraints are also applied to achieve different anisotropies by limiting one of the cone angles, $0 \leq \theta \leq 15$: (b) Conical angle $\theta_1$, (c) Conical angle $\theta_2$, and (d) Conical angle $\theta_3$.}
elements.sent-2411-403: Further design constraints can be imposed on the Pareto-optimal solutions to provide greater flexibility depending on the application.
elements.sent-2411-404: The impact of applying such constraints is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:conditional_optimal_solutions_figure .}
elements.sent-2411-405: For instance, if the application requires the structure to be as lightweight as possible, designs with low relative density may be preferred.
elements.sent-2411-406: Conversely, if maximising EA is the priority, previous insights into the structure-property relationship indicate that EA scales with relative density, with the highest EA achievable at the upper bound of relative density, specifically at $\rho = 0.6$.
elements.sent-2411-407: Additionally, suppose varying degrees of anisotropy are required.
elements.sent-2411-408: In that case, the conical angles can be constrained to smaller values, such as $0 \leq \theta \leq 15$, which influences anisotropy as described in \ref{appendix:Controlling the anisotropy of topologies .}
elements.sent-2411-409: Imposing these constraints produces a subset of optimal designs that exhibit different relationships between the objectives EA and PF.
elements.sent-2411-410: However, a notable scarcity of solutions is observed for $\theta_3$ compared to the other cases.
elements.sent-2411-411: This is because structures with anisotropy aligned parallel to the loading direction tend to be stretching-dominated, leading to high stiffness and consequently high PF.
elements.sent-2411-412: These characteristics make such structures less ideal, leading to their exclusion from the Pareto-optimal solutions, i.e. these are part of the dominated solutions that do not contribute to the total hypervolume.
elements.sent-2411-413: The few solutions that do emerge are associated with higher values for conical angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$, which enhance interconnectivity between individual structural elements, transforming the design into bending-dominated structures that are more suitable for EA applications.
elements.sent-2411-414: Subsequently, the choice would be left to the user based on their requirements, depending on the application.
elements.sent-2411-415: Nonetheless this work successfully demonstrates the application of the MOBO framework for crush energy absorption, highlighting its ability to balance multiple objectives and generate Pareto-optimal solutions, ultimately accelerating the selection of optimal structures.
elements.sent-2411-416: This versatile framework has the potential to be adapted for various other fields.
elements.sent-2411-417: For example, in aerospace engineering, where weight reduction, stiffness, and energy absorption are critical, it could be used to design lightweight structures tailored to specific mission profiles.
elements.sent-2411-418: Similarly, in biomedical engineering, the framework could optimise implants to maximise mechanical comparability with bone tissue while minimising material usage.
elements.sent-2411-419: In architectural design, it could assist in balancing structural integrity material efficiency, and aesthetics based on user preferences.
elements.sent-2411-420: Despite its successes, there are several opportunities for improving the MOBO framework.
elements.sent-2411-421: Enhancements could be made to both the FEM simulation and the optimisation process.
elements.sent-2411-422: For instance, the FEM analysis could be refined by incorporating damage initiation criteria and damage evolution to better capture fracture and progressive failure, Additionally, implementing the Hill yield model could allow for the use of material constitutive models based on short-fibre composite filaments, which are often employed in applications requiring high stiffness-to-weight ratios.
elements.sent-2411-423: Since car crashes are dynamic events and involve varying speeds, strain rate dependency could be modelled using approaches such as the Johnson-cook plasticity model, thereby increasing the model's complexity and improving the representation of topologies under load.
elements.sent-2411-424: Furthermore, the framework could be expanded to include additional objectives, such as manufacturing constraints or multi-material considerations, enabling more nuanced and application-specific designs.
elements.sent-2411-425: On the optimisation side, the process could be enhanced by implementing parallel evaluation of q-batch recommendations using array jobs on an HPC cluster.
elements.sent-2411-426: This improvement would capitalise on the ability of acquisition functions to manage multiple evaluations simultaneously, thereby accelerating the optimisation process.
elements.sent-2411-427: Future research could explore applying MOBO-based optimisation to macro-scale structures, such as large-scale models generated using spinodoid microstructures, where the reduced number of required evaluations may make this approach feasible.
elements.sent-2411-428: Additionally, leveraging BO to develop surrogate models for microscale structures could facilitate their integration into a concurrent modelling framework, where acquisition functions are tuned to explore regions of high uncertainty, rather than focusing solely on exploiting areas for more accurate surrogates.
elements.sent-2411-429: This study developed a multi-objective Bayesian optimisation framework to optimise the spinodoid design space for crush energy absorption applications.
elements.sent-2411-430: This framework enables the identification of designs that achieve a balance between competing parameters, specifically focusing on energy absorption and peak force.
elements.sent-2411-431: The process involved several key steps: First, spinodoid topologies were generated using MATLAB, with ABAQUS employed for FEM analysis.
elements.sent-2411-432: Material characterisation tests were conducted on 3D-printed PET-G samples to determine the coefficients for the anisotropic Hill material constitutive model.
elements.sent-2411-433: Various spinodoid structures were fabricated and tested to validate the FEM model.
elements.sent-2411-434: To evaluate the impact of design parameters on the objectives, two studies were conducted a one-at-a-time analysis, which measured the effect of each parameter on energy absorption and peak force, and a global Sobol' sensitivity analysis with input parameters $(\rho, \theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3)$.
elements.sent-2411-435: The results showed that all parameters significantly influenced the objectives, with a trivial relationship between relative density, $\rho$, and the objectives observed.
elements.sent-2411-436: The conical angles had less impact individually, but were more influential when varied in combination.
elements.sent-2411-437: Additionally, various MOBO-based methods were applied to solve the multi-objective problem, and the results were compared with those from the traditional NSGA-II method.
elements.sent-2411-438: After 250 iterations, the hypervolume-based qNEHVI method was found to outperform the others.
elements.sent-2411-439: The Pareto-optimal designs generally exhibited a lower peak force while maintaining energy absorption capacity, while avoiding densification through a gradient-based filter.
elements.sent-2411-440: The study demonstrated that combining FEM analysis with Bayesian optimisation is a powerful approach for solving multi-objective problems for crush energy absorption while minimising the need for costly function evaluations.

elements.subsection-2411-011: Effect of wave number on performance indicators
elements.sent-2411-448: Convergence is observed in the value of EA with an increase in the wave number due to homogenisation of the structure.
elements.sent-2411-449: The majority of the structures show a plateau beyond a wave number of, $\lambda = 12 \pi$ apart from the case of `Lamellar' structure.
elements.sent-2411-450: However, the magnitude of EA is minuscule in comparison to the rest of the test cases due to the nature of the structure, whereby a plateau in the EA may not be observed until extreme values of wave number or a higher relative density are utilised.
elements.sent-2411-451: As a result, a value of $\lambda = 15 \pi$ was chosen given the dimensions of the structures being optimised, whilst taking into account the equivalence in length scales in experiments and simulations.
elements.sent-2411-452: Nonetheless, in a scenario where the sole objective is to maximise EA, regardless of artefacts and discontinuities being generated in the structures, a low wave number should be chosen.
elements.sent-2411-453: Overall, the wave number is a homogenisation parameter, hence it should be considered as a constant within the context of the optimisation.

elements.subsection-2411-012: Effect of mesh resolution on stress-strain behaviour of spinodoid structures
elements.sent-2411-458: A mesh convergence study assessed its impact on the stress-strain behaviour of the four tested spinodoid cases.
elements.sent-2411-459: The mesh quality was controlled by varying the mesh resolution, which consequently altered the number of tetrahedral elements used to create the cellular structures.
elements.sent-2411-460: An increase in mesh resolution corresponds to a higher number of elements.
elements.sent-2411-461: Figure~\ref{fig:effect_of_resolution_on_stress_vs_strain_at_16pi illustrates the stress-strain curves for various structures generated at different mesh resolutions, ranging from 20 to 60.}
elements.sent-2411-462: Resolutions below 20 result in inadequately generated structures, while resolutions above 60 lead to excessively long simulation times.
elements.sent-2411-463: While the stress-strain behaviours of the structures are generally similar across this range, a closer inspection reveals that a mesh resolution of 20 exhibits the most significant difference.
elements.sent-2411-464: Further increases in mesh resolution result in minimal variations in behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-465: Consequently, a mesh resolution of 30 was selected for conducting the optimisation.

elements.figure-2411-036: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{MOBO_framework2.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:MOBO framework}A schematic of the multi-objective Bayesian optimisation (MOBO) process for optimising the spinodoid cellular structure design space involves four steps of the data-driven process \cite{bessa2017framework,shin2022spiderweb}. (1) Initial Dataset Creation: Sampling 50 points from the design space using the Sobol' sequence and evaluate them with FEM simulations to build the initial dataset. (2) Surrogate Model Update: Updating the Gaussian Process model based on the dataset to predict structural properties. (3) Identifying samples to evaluate: Using an acquisition function to identify and evaluate the most promising design points. (4) FEM Simulations: Performing FEM analysis on generated structures and extracting objectives to then update the dataset.}

elements.sent-2411-586: This work successfully demonstrates the application of the MOBO framework for crush energy absorption, highlighting its ability to balance multiple objectives and generate Pareto-optimal solutions, ultimately accelerating the selection of optimal structures.
elements.sent-2411-587: By acknowledging the inherent uncertainties in regions with large errors or inconsistent results (such as Columnar or Lamellar structures), these areas can be targetted with denser sampling.
elements.sent-2411-588: This approach would allow for more accurate predictions of key metrics—such as energy absorption and peak force—where the uncertainty is higher, while reducing the computational cost in regions where the model already performs well (e.g., Isotropic structures).

elements.sent-2411-594: This parallelisation would not only accelerate the overall optimisation but also allows for more comprehensive exploration of the design space, especially when combined with the targeted sampling of uncertain regions.
elements.sent-2411-595: The combination of efficient sampling based on uncertainty and parallel evaluation of batch recommendations would lead to a much faster and more accurate optimisation process.

The structures were generated at various mesh resolutions, ranging from a coarse mesh resolution of 20 to a fine mesh resolution of 60. }
elements.sent-2411-616: ABAQUS Explicit requires stable time increment $\Delta t = L^e/C_d$ to obtain a stable solution, where $L^e$ is the characteristic element length defined by the smallest element used to mesh the geometry, and $C_d = \sqrt{E/\rho}$ is the wave propagation speed, with $E$ being the Young's modulus and density of the material, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-617: For quasi-static speed at which the spinodoids were tested, the stable time dictates the number of increments required to solve the problem.
elements.sent-2411-618: Time scaling allows a reduction of the number of increments required as number of increments = $t / \Delta t$, where $t$ is the total time period.
elements.sent-2411-619: In this case, the computation time reduces as much as the time was reduced by artificially increasing the velocity.
elements.sent-2411-620: In contrast, the stable time increment can also be increased, which is called mass scaling, whereby the density is artificially increased.
elements.sent-2411-621: This decreases the computation time by the square root of the density.
elements.sent-2411-622: The analysis was conducted using the geometries described in Fig.~\ref{fig:4 spinodoid types and compared with the corresponding experimental results, as shown in \ref{fig:PET_G_exp_validation_time_scaling .}}
elements.sent-2411-623: For the `Columnar' structure, increasing the total time period led to a rapid reduction in initial stiffness, and a decrease in random fluctuations in stress values.
elements.sent-2411-624: Additionally, the time required for stress values to increase after contact between the structure and the loader measured by the stationary anvil was reduced, resulting in a better match of the small-strain response with experimental data, particularly at higher total time periods.
elements.sent-2411-625: However, the initial stiffness became more consistent with experimental results as the total time period increased.
elements.sent-2411-626: Similar trends were observed for the `Cubic' and `Lamellar' structures.
elements.sent-2411-627: For the `Cubic' structure, stress values at large strains began to deviate from experimental results at longer time periods.
elements.sent-2411-628: In contrast, for the `Lamellar' structure, the stress-strain response at high strain values aligned more closely with experimental data as the total time period increased.
elements.sent-2411-629: Based on these observations, it was essential to balance simulation accuracy and computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-630: At shorter total time periods, the higher loading velocity increases kinetic energy, making inertia effects more prominent.
elements.sent-2411-631: This introduces unintended strain dependency, leading to a non-linear small-strain response.
elements.sent-2411-632: However, shorter time periods also significantly reduce computation time.
elements.sent-2411-633: To achieve an optimal balance, a total time period of 0.002 seconds was selected.
elements.figure-2411-049: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{PET_G_exp_validation_time_scaling.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:PET_G_exp_validation_time_scaling} \textcolor{black}{Plot showing the effect of time scaling for each test geometry described in Fig.~\ref{fig:4 spinodoid types} on the stress-strain response of each structure obtained from FEM simulations. The behaviour of each structure was tested under three different total time periods shown by the green, red, and blue lines indicating time periods of 0.001, 0.002, and 0.005 seconds, respectively, while the different black lines show the results from experiment.}}
elements.sent-2411-634: Three different fixed mass scaling (FMS) values were tested, showing minimal differences in the force-displacement curves, indicating no significant loss of accuracy as illustrated in \ref{fig:mass_scaling_v2 .}
elements.sent-2411-635: Increasing the FMS value led to a reduction in total simulation time, with an increase of 0.5 resulting in a 14\% reduction in computation time.
elements.sent-2411-636: However, it also slightly reduced the energy absorption calculated from the force-displacement curve.
elements.sent-2411-637: Further increases in the FMS value continued to reduce simulation time, from 981 seconds at an FMS value of 1 to 636 seconds at a value of 2.
elements.sent-2411-638: Despite these performance gains, the energy absorption values showed only a partial reduction.
elements.sent-2411-639: However, artificially increasing the density (a consequence of mass scaling) reduced the wave speed, delaying the reaction force's propagation through the structure.
elements.sent-2411-640: This delay caused an initial lag in the force-displacement curve before stress values began to rise.
elements.sent-2411-641: Ultimately, an FMS value of 2 was selected, as it provided a balance between reduced simulation time and acceptable accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-642: Further increases in FMS resulted in diminished the accuracy of the mechanical response, making higher values unsuitable.

The plot on the left shows the effect of three different levels of mass scaling on the mechanical response using force-displacement graph, while the plot on the right shows its effect on the simulation time and the calculated energy absorption value.}}
